metadata.title: Images of controversy: Examining cognition of hydraulic fracturing among policy elites and the general public

abstract:
Highly contested energy and environmental policy issues are often characterized by entrenched policy debates and inconsistent or failed policies. This study examines the cognition of polarized policy positions in an effort to explain what factors influence the mental models' individuals use to think about hydraulic fracturing and adopted policy positions on the controversial issue of hydraulic fracturing. The study first examines the role of affect on the use of "cognitive policy images"

or the individual-level mental models used to represent policy positions on hydraulic fracturing using structural topic modeling. Next, regression analysis is used to estimate the effects of theoretically relevant factors on cognition. While previous studies have focused on public attitudes, this study takes a comparative approach to examine policy elite and public cognition around this controversial issue. A comparative approach not only points to subtle differences in how elites and members of the general public cognitively process this controversial policy issue but also provides some insight into how political sophistication factors into cognition and influences policy positions on hydraulic fracturing practices.

paragraphs: Hydraulic fracturing (HF) is a highly contested issue in the U.S. and globally (Millar, 2020). While HF is a complex, multifaceted, and highly technical process it is often conceptualized as being either positive or negative. More specifically, subsystem debates around HF consist of proponents who attribute HF with positive outcomes commonly associated with energy security, job creation, and regional economic benefits (Federal Multiagency Collaboration on Unconventional Oil and Gas Research: A Strategy for Research and Development, 2014;Greenstone, 2018). This view is contrasted with opponents who cite HF's negative impacts on the environment and human health (Stockton, 2015; US EPA National Center for Environmental Assessment and Frithsen 2015; Vengosh et al., 2013). The implications of these debates are worth consideration.

Decades of contrasting media frames (Heikkila et al., 2014) and polarized policy positions (Weible & Heikkila, 2017) have given way to sustained debates, resulting in inconsistent (Zirogiannis et al., 2016) and failed policies (Weible et al., 2017). Arguably, the enduring policy debates over HF have normalized contrasting mental frames that oversimplify the issue as being positive and worth supporting or negative and something to be avoided. As a result, the dichotomous framing of the issue has been used in numerous cyber-based disinformation campaigns whereby competing frames are exploited with motivations to further polarize the issue (Old Tactics, New Tools: A Review of Russia's Soft Cyber Influence Operations, 2017; U.S. House of Representatives, 2018) and destabilize the U.S. energy market (U.S. House of Representatives, 2018). Sustained disagreements over the outcomes of HF have, therefore, significantly impacted other substantive policy areas including national security and foreign policy among others. Previous studies have identified several factors that support ongoing policy debates over HF.

A more comprehensive understanding of the process by which individuals form positions on this policy issue is critical. Such knowledge is essential for overcoming entrenched debates and pursuing viable policy solutions. Current research focuses predominately on public attitudes toward HF. Public support for HF activity appears bound to an awareness of the competing economic and environmental dimensions (Pierce et al., 2018) with political affiliation, proximity to HF activity, and familiarity with the technology each playing some role in the formation of public perceptions (Borick et al., 2014;Boudet et al., 2014;Gottlieb et al., 2018;Rabe & Borick, 2011, 2013). In the U.S. context, it is reasonable to examine public opinions toward this issue but much less is known about the attitudes and decision-making among those more influential in the policymaking process. A rare study of coalition policy actors found that conflicting policy narratives are used to emphasize economic and environmental dimensions of HF as well (Weible & Heikkila, 2017).

Consistent with normative expectations, evidence supports that contrasting policy positions function to support sustained conflict (Berardo et al., 2020;Yordy et al., 2019), but it is not yet clear how or why contrasting positions form. Applied to the entrenched debates around HF, why are particular dimensions of this issue cognitively isolated and prioritized? Furthermore, within the context of democratic policymaking, conflicting views held by the public as well as those more engaged in the political process are likely to have a significant effect on policymaking around this issue. How might differences in political sophistication impact policy positions on HF? This study offers a rare opportunity. Expanding on existing research, which focused on public attitudes toward HF, this study examines cognition around contrasting policy positions held by policy elites and members of the public. paragraphs: Cognition involves processing sensory input using affect to simplify, store, recover, and elaborate on mental images that represent more complex concepts (Duncan & Barrett, 2007). Affect is most commonly characterized as a positively or negatively valenced emotion (Barrett & Bliss-Moreau, 2009). Under conditions of uncertainty, stemming from either ambiguity or complexity, affective responses can be at once both positive and negative (Anderson et al., 2019) and it is under these conditions that context (Anderson et al., 2019) is likely to guide judgments (Barrett & Bliss-Moreau, 2009). Applied to understand how cognitive processing may factor into the formation of policy positions around HF, it is likely that affect is a necessary component in policy-oriented cognition. The valenced nature of affective processing tends to support dichotomous thinking or cognitive distortion (Knapp & Beck, 2008) which involves a rigid processing of information that relies on beliefs to support decision making under conditions of ambiguity or uncertainty (Bonfá-Araujo et al., 2021) by reducing complex constructs into contrasting categories for organizing information (Berlin, 1990). A number of previous studies have reported contrasting policy positions toward HF that emphasize environmental and economic outcomes related to HF. Affect-driven cognition would explain the competing nature of policy positions on HF activity. It is expected that the communication of the mental models cognitively retained by individuals to represent their policy position on HF (cognitive policy images) will rely on affective processing.
Hypothesis 1 In the context of HF, it is expected that affect-driven cognition will produce contrasting policy positions that reflect economic and environmental dimensions of HF.

If the valenced nature of affective processing supports the cognitive selection of simplified, contrasting policy positions then what explains why economic and environmental dimensions of HF, in particular, are cognitively selected to think about this policy issue? Under conditions of uncertainty, affective judgments are particularly reliant on contextual information (Anderson et al., 2019). The next section briefly introduces theoretical frameworks that identify which contextual factors are likely to play a fundamental role in how individuals deal with uncertainty to identify threats or opportunities and organize policy positions.

sections.header_raw: Culturally biased beliefs, cognition, and policy positions
sections.header_norm: culturally biased beliefs cognition and policy positions
paragraphs: Shared beliefs drive policy interactions and policy change (Jenkins-Smith, Nohrstedt, et al., 2014) through opposing coalitions that subscribe to divergent perceptions of policy issues (Weible & Heikkila, 2017). Grid Group Cultural Theory (GGCT) has been applied in multiple policy studies to examine the importance of broadly defined beliefs in policy change (Ripberger et al., 2014;Swedlow, 2014;Swedlow & Wyckoff, 2009;Zanocco et al., 2018). Culturally biased beliefs like those specified by GGCT, have also been found to facilitate the mental organization of policyrelevant concepts (Jones & Song, 2014) and contribute to the persuasiveness of some communications (Shanahan et al., 2011) particularly when it involves selecting between binary or contrasting communications of an issue (Brewer & Gross, 2005;Shanahan et al., 2011;Sniderman & Theriault, 2018). A closer review of GGCT offers some explanation for why certain attributes of policy issues might garner attention.

Based on work by Mary Douglas to explain social conflict, GGCT posits that that shared values and beliefs inform our social interactions, preferences, judgments, attention to dangers, and choices in life (Douglas & Wildavsky, 1982;Swedlow, 2014;Thompson et al., 1990;Wildavsky, 1987;Wildavsky & Dake, 1990). Shared beliefs specified in this theoretical framework determine the extent to which regulations (grid) and social relationships (group) constrain behaviors, resulting in four distinct worldviews that guide decision making (Thompson et al., 1990;Wildavsky, 1987). The value placed on grid and group dimensions form four prototypical worldviews that encompass beliefs about the environment, the economy, human nature, and risk (Gastil, 2006;Jenkins-Smith, Silva, et al., 2014;Ripberger et al., 2011Ripberger et al., , 2012Ripberger et al., , 2014;;Thompson et al., 1990;Wildavsky & Dake, 1990). Prototypical egalitarians (low-grid, high-group) value strong social solidarity and collective decision-making but reject institutionally imposed regulations. Interactions with the environment demand careful consideration as egalitarians tend to view the environment as fragile and vulnerable while energy technologies are viewed as an extreme threat to the fragile environment (Moyer & Song, 2016, 2019). Contrastingly, prototypical individualists (low-grid and low-group) view the environment as self-sufficient and resilient. This renders them secure in seeking economic opportunities while rejecting constraints from either institutionally or socially based sources. As a result, they are unlikely to acknowledge the risks associated with technological experimentation, particularly if it is associated with economic opportunity (Moyer & Song, 2016, 2019). Prototypical hierarchs (high-grid and high-group) value institutionalized authority. This constrains individualistic behavior and encourages deference to experts and authorities which translates to interactions with the environment which they believe requires institutionalized management. They are predisposed to trust technological innovations as long as they are managed by what hierarchs view as a legitimate institution. In the case where management of the environment is institutionalized, prototypical hierarchs are predisposed to acknowledge opportunities. Cultural theory also describes fatalists (high-grid, low-group) as those who prototypically lack social connection although they feel bound by institutional authority. This results in a general disengagement from many issues, including energy technologies (Moyer & Song, 2016, 2019). For this reason, fatalists are not considered in this study. In summary, culturally biased beliefs are theorized to guide individual judgment in ways that support socially constructed views of the world. Culturally biased beliefs are expected to provide the contextual guidance for judgments regarding HF. Given the distinct characterizations of the natural world and unique predispositions for selective attention toward risks and opportunities, three types of culturally biased beliefs as specified by GGCT are expected to provide relevant contextual information that informs cognition and the resulting policy positions around HF.

Hypothesis 2 It is expected that strong egalitarianism will guide selective attention toward environmental outcomes, emphasizing the (negative) threats that HF poses to what is believed to be a fragile environment over any (positive) economic outcomes.

Hypothesis 3 It is expected that strong individualism will guide selective attention toward economic outcomes, emphasizing the (positive) opportunities created by HF over any (negative) environmental outcomes due to their belief in a self-stabilizing environment.

Hypothesis 4 It is expected that strong hierarchism will guide selective attention toward economic outcomes, emphasizing the (positive) opportunities created by HF over any (negative) environmental outcomes due to a belief that institutional management of environmental threats is sufficient for supporting equilibrium in the environment.

As beliefs are expected to play a fundamental role in the cognitive selection of policy positions toward HF, it is important to acknowledge the ambiguity associated with the relationship between knowledge and beliefs. This study grants further consideration to the concept of knowledge given its influence in cognition and attitude formation particularly under conditions of intense affect (Biek et al., 1996).

paragraphs: Attitudinal studies within the context of the U.S. naturally evoke concepts that underly democratic principles. Conceptually, the public's positions on policies are shaped by policy elites (Baumgartner & Mahoney, 2008;Jones & Baumgartner, 2005). More specifically, policy images are curated within political institutions (Kirp, 1982) or coalitions (Weible et al., 2020) with intentions to expand interest within policy subsystems (Sabatier & Jenkins-Smith, 1999;True et al., 2007;Weible et al., 2009). As a result, pertinent distinctions between policy elites and the general public with regard to their political knowledge have emerged from this research. Studies suggest that policy elites tend to be more educated, more informed, and more experienced than the public with regard to policy issues (Chin et al., 2000).

The relationship between political knowledge, beliefs, and the formation of policy attitudes has been debated among scholars. Some conclude that low levels of political sophistication give way to inconsistent political belief systems and incoherent policy preferences (Carpini et al., 1997;Converse, 1964;Michaud et al., 2009;Stimson, 1975;Zaller, 1992). On the other hand, some evidence suggests that consistent worldviews are not dependent on knowledge (Ripberger et al., 2012) and beliefs inform policy preferences regardless of knowledge levels (Goren, 2004;Popkin, 1991). These studies point to the complex nature of knowledge and illustrate some general challenges surrounding its operationalization.

Other approaches examine the impact of knowledge and beliefs in processing policy-relevant information. For example, political knowledge that is coupled with strong culturally oriented value predispositions has been found to increase the influential nature of certain communication strategies (Jorgensen et al., 2018). Similarly, some requisite knowledge was found to be important for interpreting, storing, and retrieving political information to facilitate choices that reconciled with previously held beliefs (Miller & Krosnick, 2000, p. 312). Robust knowledge is thought to ease comprehension (Nelson et al., 1997;Nelson & Oxley, 1999;Zaller, 1985Zaller, , 1992) ) although under certain conditions, the effects of knowledge may diminish (Druckman & Nelson, 2003;Zaller, 1992).

To summarize, inconsistencies in the operationalization of political knowledge confound a clear understanding of the relationship between beliefs and knowledge in the context of policy. With that said, evidence is building to suggest that beliefs provide a fundamental and consistent basis for processing policy-relevant information and forming preferences. More intricate knowledge of political processes or substantively relevant information may facilitate cognitive reasoning in ways that support existing beliefs. In an effort to isolate the effects of beliefs on the formation of policy positions, this study attempts to account for multiple facets of knowledge under a broader concept of political sophistication. A comparative approach can highlight similarities and differences between policy elites, those with relatively high levels of political sophistication, and the general public. Therefore, beliefs are expected to inform the policy positions of both policy elites and the general public. Additionally, higher levels of political sophistication among policy elites are expected to ease the cognitive selection of contrasting outcomes associated with HF.
Hypo thesis 5 Culturally biased beliefs will influence policy positions for both policy elites and the general public in ways that are consistent with H2-H4.

sections.paper_id: Moyer - 2022 - Images of controversy Examining cognition of hydraulic fracturing among policy elites and the gener
paragraphs: The data analyzed in this study was recorded in two states located within the U.S. that represent contrasting experiences with regard to unconventional fuel extraction using HF. The data was collected using an Internet-based survey focusing on local energy policy issues. The sample contains local policy elites and members of the general public located in two states, Arkansas and Oregon, with contrasting experience in HF. The state of Arkansas contains one of the largest shale gas formations in the U.S. and, at the time of the survey had recorded more than 4000 active fractured wells as well as a moratorium in place on permanent disposal wells in designated areas (Davenport, 2015). Contrastingly, Oregon recorded no extractive activities and had proposed a bill that would prohibit the use of HF practices for any possible future recovery (Fahey et al., 2019). The survey was conducted in two phases between 2015 and 2017. Email invitations included a short description and an embedded link to the survey. Emails of local policy elites were obtained in both states from publicly available municipal and relevant professional websites. 1 A representative sample of the general public in both states were collected through Qualtrics and include individuals residing in 50 cities in Arkansas and 150 cities in Oregon. Completed surveys (1396 in Arkansas and 5384 in Oregon) were processed for incomplete data resulting in 1152 total responses (n = 322 for policy elites and n = 830 for the general public). The make-up of the general public sample is similar to characteristics of the U.S. population in that they are male (49.6% sample vs. 49% U.S. population) but contain more white/non-Hispanics (84.6% sample vs. 76.6% U.S. population) (U.S. Census Bureau QuickFacts, 2016). After removing incomplete responses, the same reflects 322 responses from local policy elites and 830 responses from the general public. Before introducing the measures used, justifications and conceptualizations associated with local policy elites are briefly discussed.

paragraphs: Consistent with Elgin & Weible (Elgin & Weible, 2013), policy elites in this study are conceptualized as individuals who possess the resources and ability to influence policy at the local level through specialized roles within the policy process and who are engaged in energy policy issues.

Theoretically, policy elites are particularly relevant to the policy process. Certain attributes of policy actors within a policy subsystem are useful for understanding how policy conflicts are sustained (Heikkila & Weible, 2017). Furthermore, economic elites and organized groups have been found to exert a remarkable influence on policy formation (Gilens & Page, 2014). In the context of the policy debate concerning HF, it is assumed that local policy elites play a pivotal role in the ongoing debate. For this study, local policy elites include mayors, city council members, and municipal leaders, as well as non-profit organization affiliates.

sections.header_raw: Extracting policy positions using structural topic modeling
sections.header_norm: extracting policy positions using structural topic modeling
paragraphs: It is expected that affect-driven cognition, the mechanism by which mental models are constructed for thinking about policy issues, informs individual-level policy positions. Structural topic modeling (STM) using the stm package was run in an R computing environment (Roberts et al., 2018(Roberts et al., , 2013(Roberts et al., , 2014)). This method was chosen because the method compliments affect-driven cognitive processing as theorized. STM uses a semi-unsupervised learning approach within a machine learning scheme to infer topics for each open-ended response based on the distribution of words represented by a semantic theme (K) using a mixed-membership model. Because estimations are sensitive to the distribution over words for a particular topic, "Spectral" initialization was used (Roberts et al., 2018). STM allows for the incorporation of relevant metadata or covariates to estimate meaningful variation present in the frequency with which a topic is discussed (topical prevalence) and within the words chosen to describe or discuss a particular topic (topical content). In this analysis, the covariate chosen is affect.

Cognitive mental images, which are anchored to an individual's real-world experiences, are routinely expressed semantically through the use of words that operate as formalized symbols representing an individual's "realm of reality" (Harder, 1954, p. 53) and guides the analysis. The first step is to operationalize affect, the foundation of affect-driven cognition, by asking respondents to indicate how they generally feel about fracking on a scale of one (extremely negative) to seven (extremely positive). Next, the mental models used to represent policy positions on HF (cognitive policy image) is measured using an open-ended survey question, when you think about fracking, what is the first image that comes to mind? This approach allows for completely unstructured responses that are intended to avoid a priori researcher-specified assessment. Respondents' semantic expression of cognitive policy images used to mentally represent HF in the context of energy policy is a window into how individuals cognitively organize information about the issue as restricted by processing considerations (Kahneman, 2003;Kahneman et al., 1982;Kahneman & Tversky, 1979;Tversky & Kahneman, 1992) including affective processing (Damasio et al., 1996). STM is applied to analyze whether affect-driven cognitive processing produces policy positions that reflect economic and environmental dimensions of HF. The variable affect is used in STM to infer topical importance and uses the frequency and uniqueness of words to extract latent topics from unstructured data. Topical inference is used to summarize extracted topics through the calculation of prioritized words (those words that have the highest frequency of use for a given topic as calculated in various ways). Semantic patterns extracted to represent latent categories or cognitive (mental) policy images in this analysis were extracted using overall frequency and exclusivity (FREX in stm), lift weights, and the log frequency of the word in the primary topic compared to the log frequency of the word in other topics (Score in stm) (Roberts et al., 2018).

sections.header_raw: Estimating effects of beliefs on policy positions
sections.header_norm: estimating effects of beliefs on policy positions
paragraphs: To test whether culturally biased beliefs influence the policy positions held by policy elites and the public, OLS regression is used. To operationalize policy positions, policy positions are measured as a continuous variable ranging from 0 to 1 reflecting the proportion of the openended response that corresponds with a specific cognitive (mental) policy image. While descriptive images were also extracted using STM, economic, and environmental cognitive policy images are of primary concern for this study given the findings of previous studies. Policy positions are coded as economic or environmental based on the results of STM analysis. As a result, the DV for this analysis is a continuous variable. For example, a hypothetical response could be comprised of semantic patterns corresponding to environmental cognitive policy images and measure 0.54. The same response could hypothetically also contain semantic patterns associated with economic cognitive policy images measuring 0.13. In this case, the response would indicate a reliance on environmental cognitive policy image and measure 0.54 for environmental policy position. Conversely, the hypothetical response would measure 0.13 for economic policy position.

The primary IV of culturally biased beliefs is operationalized using GGCT specified measures used in previous studies (e.g., Jenkins-Smith, Silva, et al., 2014;Ripberger et al., 2014;Song et al., 2014). A total of three survey questions corresponding to three cultural worldviews (i.e., egalitarianism, individualism, and hierarchism) result in a total of nine culturally nuanced statements (provided in random order in the survey) rated one to seven, with one indicating that the respondent strongly disagrees and seven indicating strong agreement. Factor analysis (with the varimax rotation method) reveals three latent factors aligning with the three distinctive dimensions theoretically defined. Related measures exhibit consistently high factor loadings (i.e., factor loading greater than 0.5) while unrelated factors exhibit low loadings. Based upon this factor structure, factor scores are used as an index for measuring each cultural orientation to create a numeric variable. Cronbach's α scores for the three survey items (constituting each GGCT index) among policy elites range from 0.65 to 0.81 and 0.66 to 0.79 for the general public indicating that the related survey measures are reasonably reliable. Contrasting policy positions serve as the DV and are operationalized using STM output results.

Given the political polarization of HF, political party identification is measured by respondents indicating whether they identify with the Democratic or Republican party. Demographic information such as age, gender, race, education, income, are also measured and serve as control variables (see Table 1).

sections.header_norm: extracting policy positions using structural topic modeling
paragraphs: It is hypothesized that affect-driven cognition, the mechanism by which mental models are constructed for thinking about policy issues, informs individual-level policy positions. Using the affect variable as a covariate to estimate meaningful variation present in the frequency with which a topic is discussed (topical prevalence) and within the words chosen to describe or discuss a particular topic (topical content), STM was used to extract latent topics which are displayed in Table 4. 2 The analytical results reveal similarities and distinctions present in the semantic patterns of policy elite and general public recorded cognitive policy images on HF. The first extracted topic (Latent Topic 1 as displayed in Table 4) is best characterized by terms like water, earthquake, pollution, fracking, damage, environment, and destruction. Both policy elites and the public frequently use these terms to describe the policy image used to think about HF. The proportion of the response attributable to this topic on average is 39% among the public and 46% among policy elites (see Table 2). A review of representative responses (see Table 5) provides a more detailed description of cognitive policy images. Concern with damage to the environment as a result of water pollution and earthquakes are most common among both policy elites and members of the public. Primary distinctions between images used by elites and the public seem to relate to more specific knowledge of the HF process among elites. For example, public concerns for water quality reference water in general terms while policy elites specify groundwater. Extracted topics

for both groups associate environmental damage and destruction with HF practices, therefore, Latent Topic 1 is coded as Environmental Cognitive Policy Image.

With regard to the second latent topic, both elites and the public use the term energy to think about HF. An examination of representative responses reveals that both perceive of energy as existing in abundance. Jobs, energy independence, and economic growth are all outcomes associated with HF activity for policy elites. The general public also associates energy independence with HF but acknowledges physical changes that result from HF in ways that policy elites do not. The average proportion of response attributable to this topic is 35% among the public and 36% among policy elites (see Table 2). Latent Topic 2 is coded Economic Cognitive Policy Image.

A third latent topic is characterized by the frequent use of terms like drill, extract, and break to describe actions associated with HF practices. This topic is the least common among the three extracted topics making up an average of only 26% of the responses for the public and 18% for policy elites (see Table 2). Additionally, locations for these actions are noted occurring in rock, near wells, and underground. Both policy elites and the general public use descriptive images to think about HF with no meaningful distinctions detected. Given previous findings highlighting the significance of environmental and economic dimensions of this issue and the theoretical expectations posited for this study, further analysis is confined to latent topics 1 and 2 only.

In this first step of analysis, the analytical results provide evidence to support the expectations stated in H1. Using measured affect to estimate the variation captured in open-ended responses compliments affect-driven cognition. This procedure produced two latent topics that utilize semantics which closely resemble contrasting positions on HF reported in other studies. This was true for policy elites and the public. The semantic patterns representing environmental outcomes are nearly identical for elites and the general public. Distinct semantic patterns are prominent in the economic outcomes provided. Additionally, a third topic indicates that elites and the public may also think about HF in more general descriptive terms and share some common semantic patterns. The next stage of analysis examines whether culturally biased beliefs function as contextual guides for forming contrasting policy positions.

This procedure produced two latent topics that utilize semantics which closely resemble contrasting positions on HF reported in other studies. This was true for policy elites and the public. The semantic patterns representing environmental outcomes are nearly identical for elites and the general public. Distinct semantic patterns are prominent in the economic outcomes provided. Additionally, a third topic indicates that elites and the public may also think about HF in more general descriptive terms and share some common semantic patterns. The next stage of analysis examines whether culturally biased beliefs function as contextual guides for forming contrasting policy positions.

Using the affect variable as a covariate to estimate meaningful variation present in the frequency with which a topic is discussed (topical prevalence) and within the words chosen to describe or discuss a particular topic (topical content), STM was used to extract latent topics which are displayed in Table 4. 2 The analytical results reveal similarities and distinctions present in the semantic patterns of policy elite and general public recorded cognitive policy images on HF. The first extracted topic (Latent Topic 1 as displayed in Table 4) is best characterized by terms like water, earthquake, pollution, fracking, damage, environment, and destruction.

paragraphs: It is expected that culturally biased beliefs inform cognition of HF and in doing so, influence resulting policy positions. To test this, OLS regression modeling is used in an additive fashion to estimate the effects of beliefs while controlling for other relevant variables. Demographics (Boudet et al., 2014(Boudet et al., , 2018)), partisan factors (Boudet et al., 2014;Howell et al., 2017), and proximity to HF activities (Alcorn et al., 2017;Boudet et al., 2018) have been found to influence attitudes toward HF. For this reason, demographic factors, political party identification, as well as state and year are incorporated into the regression model to statistically control for these factors. Regression coefficients are displayed in Table 6. Regression coefficients contained in models 1 and 3 display the effects of partisanship and demographic factors on respondents' selective attention to environmental and economic outcomes of HF. Age, race, education, income, state, and year variables are all incorporated into the regression model but because their effects were not statistically significant, they are not displayed in Table 6 for the sake of clarity. Of the demographic variables, gender is the only variable to show a statistically significant effect. Males are more likely to direct attention to the economic outcomes of HF (+0.214, p-value < .05 for policy elites and +0.066, p-value < .05 for the general public) over environmental outcomes (-0.169, p-value < .05 for policy elites and -0.100, p-value < .05 for the general public). Effects associated with political party identification is also statistically significant. Republican-affiliated elites are more likely to think about economic outcomes (0.059, p-value < .05) as are members of the general public (0.032, p-value < .05). Contrastingly, elites (-0.053, p-value < .05) and members of the general public (-0.041, p-value < .05) who identify with Democrats are more likely to pay attention to environmental outcomes. Displaying the regression results in an additive manner highlights the effects of gender and partisanship as distinct from that of culturally biased beliefs. The primary IV exhibits significant effects on the DV (adjusted R 2 value increases from .0115 and 0.154 to 0.323 and 0.388 for policy elites and from 0.060 and 0.055 to 0.164 and 0.140 in the general public). The direction supports theoretical expectations. More specifically, H2 is supported by the regression results indicating that strong egalitarianism is positively associated with environmental outcomes for both policy elites (+0.117, p-value < .05) and the general public (+0.060, p-value < .05) and negatively associated with economic outcomes (-0.136, p-value < .05 for elites and -0.037, p-value < .05 for the public). Conversely, individualism is positively associated with economic outcomes among policy elites (+0.106, p-value < .05) and the public (+0.034, p-value < .05) and negatively associated with environmental outcomes (-0.087, p-value < .05 for policy elites and -0.052, p-value < 0.05 for the general public). This supports H3. Expectations reflected in H4 are partially supported as the effects of hierarchism are statistically significant for the public only. Statistically, significant regression coefficients show that hierarchism among policy elites is positively associated with economic outcomes (+0.016, p-value < .05) and negatively associated with environmental outcomes (-0.024, p-value < .05). For the general public, identification with the Republican party remained statistically significant for explaining attention toward economic outcomes while controlling for all other variables although some explanatory power was lost (dropped from 0.032, p-value < .05 in model 1 to 0.016, p-value < .05 in model 2).

The results generally support the expectation stated in H5. Strong egalitarian and individualist beliefs inform policy positions for both policy elites and the general public as evidenced by the results detailed above. A notable exception is reflected in the analytical results related

sections.paper_id: Moyer - 2022 - Images of controversy Examining cognition of hydraulic fracturing among policy elites and the gener
paragraphs: Previous studies have noted the contrasting views surrounding the use of HF and found evidence suggesting that positions on the issue commonly feature positive economic outcomes in the form of energy security, job creation, and regional economic growth contrasted with negative environmental and health outcomes. These opposing views are believed to sustain continued policy debates. Expanding on previous studies, this study examines factors emerging from policy process theories and theories of cognition to better understand how and why contrasting attributes of HF outcomes are incorporated into policy positions. By comparing local policy elites with the general public, this study provides a unique perspective with which to assess how political sophistication impacts individual-level judgment.

The analysis begins with an examination of the cognitive (mental) policy images used by policy elites and the general public to think and communicate about HF. Synthesizing theoretical frameworks applied to understand the policy process with theories of cognition, this study tests whether contrasting policy positions may emerge from processing inherent to cognition under conditions of uncertainty. Cognition distortion (Knapp & Beck, 2008) is a reliance on contrasting constructs to organize information (Berlin, 1990). It is affect-driven and a somewhat rigid way of processing that leverages bipolarized aspects of an issue in cognitively useful ways that support decision making (Bonfá-Araujo et al., 2021). The process involves using affective feelings to assign valence to mental images as a way to tag, store, and recall information. Recalling H1, this process of affect-driven cognition is expected to produce contrasting policy positions on HF which reflect the positive economic and negative environmental outcomes recorded in numerous previous studies. STM was used to extract latent topics using affective feelings toward HF and found that both policy elites and the general public use cognitive images to form broadly defined policy positions in similar ways. Based on these results, it follows that the uncertainty of outcomes from HF compels individuals to simplify their understanding of the issue using affect-driven cognition which directs individuals' selective attention toward contrasting economic and environmental dimensions of HF. As described earlier, context also plays an important role in this process and will be addressed in the following discussions around H2-H4.

While not the primary focus of the study, it is worth noting that in addition to contrasting economic and environmental policy concerns, the analysis also revealed the presence of nonpolarized, non-valenced attributes embedded within the respondents' policy positions. While much less common in its usage, the descriptive attributes of HF are also used to think about and communicate about HF. This result may hold some practical importance. The identification of uncontested areas of an issue are an important step in overcoming cognitive conflict and building consensus (Lubell, 2000). It is not possible to know from this study how neutral dimensions might factor into consensus building on HF but these results point to an interesting area for future inquiry

Expanding on earlier studies, this research analyzes theoretically relevant predictors of the broadly defined policy positions on HF just discussed. Consistent with Boudet et al.'s (2014) findings, gender and political ideology were found to have some influence on HF policy positions.

As stated in H2-H4, culturally biased beliefs are expected to guide selective cognitive attention toward certain attributes of the policy issue. As discussed previously, affect-driven cognition also relies on context (Anderson et al., 2019) to guide judgments (Barrett & Bliss-Moreau, 2009). Aligned with GGCT which posits that that culturally shared values and beliefs inform preferences and judgments (Douglas & Wildavsky, 1982;Swedlow, 2014;Thompson et al., 1990;Wildavsky, 1987;Wildavsky & Dake, 1990), GGCT was used to operationalize beliefs. The results of OLS regression analysis indicate that culturally biased beliefs have significantly distinct effects consistent with theoretical expectations. Supporting H2, individuals with strong egalitarian values are more likely to rely on cognitive policy images that relate to concern for the environmental outcomes of HF. Applying a cultural theory lens, this can be explained by foundational concerns for the natural environment that lie central to egalitarian beliefs. Egalitarianism guides selective attention to the threats that HF poses to a fragile environment. This threat is commonly represented by specific mental images of polluted water and earthquakes and interpreted as signs of a fragile environment on the brink of collapse. Because egalitarian values support a strong distrust of industry, government, and institutionally supported regulatory frameworks, any regulatory activities are likely to be interpreted as futile.

Analytical results also provide some support for the expectations contained in H3. Policy elites and members of the public who exhibit an affinity for individualism are predisposed to cognitively select policy positions that identify with the economic outcomes of HF as reflected by economic cognitive policy images. GGCT identifies views of a robust environment as core to individualistic pursuits so strong individualists are most likely to view HF as an economic opportunity. Therefore, they need not be concerned about the environmental impacts of HF. Their disconnection from institutional authority coupled with their perspective of the environment mean they are unlikely to assign any value to environmental regulation. In stark contrast to egalitarian predispositions, individualist beliefs characterize HF as an opportunity for economic growth.

Partial support was found for H4. The results indicate that among the general public, hierarchism is likely to guide cognitive attention toward the economic outcomes of HF. Insight from GGCT indicates that the motivations for hierarchies are quite distinct from that of strong individualists. Strong hierarchism retains concern for environmental outcomes but also possesses strong inclinations to defer to institutional authority. The existence of industry standards and governmental regulatory schemes are more likely to reassure those individuals who hold hierarch values. Belief that the environmental impacts can be managed reserves space for those with an affinity for hierarchism to cognitively focus on economic considerations. Environmental considerations are assuaged but the need for management is likely to counter some of the enthusiasm for pursuing economic outcomes of HF when compared to individualism. This explains the stronger predispositions toward economic-focused policy positions associated with individualism when compared to hierarchism.

Unexpectedly, hierarchism did not exhibit statistically significant effects on the policy positions of local policy elites. The regression coefficient for policy elites holding hierarch-type beliefs indicates a direction consistent with theoretical expectations but was not statistically significant as it was for members of the public. Policy elites' substantive knowledge of HF and the political processes surrounding environmental regulation may ease cognition in ways described earlier but this does not explain the findings for policy elites with strong hierarch values. GGCT places both egalitarianism and individualism low in their value of institutional rules. For both of these belief systems, the environmental outcomes of HF are much clearer, and regulatory approaches to manage HF are likely to be viewed as inadequate in affecting the outcomes. Contrastingly, the environmental outcomes for strong hierarchs are less certain. Hierarch preferences for the management of environmental outcomes are distinct from other cultural typologies. Some evidence suggests that in addition to values, administrative and bureaucratic considerations may affect policy decision making (Newman et al., 2016). Perhaps these considerations hold some priority for policy elites with strong hierarch values. Looking at the use of policy research in policymaking, belief-based assimilation of policy information among bureaucrats has become increasingly limited (Caplan, 1979;Dunn, 1980;Webber, 1987). While outside the scope of this research, further examination of the relationship between cultural beliefs, cognition, and perceived administrative or bureaucratic roles could provide a more nuanced look at how beliefs and cognition influence policy elites' decision-making.

Based on these findings, it is likely that the uncertainty surrounding HF outcomes create conditions for cognition that are particularly reliant on broad, culturally biased worldviews, or beliefs to further direct affect-driven cognitive awareness. As a result, particular socially constructed and culturally reinforced sensitivities direct selective attention toward HF economic and environmental dimensions of HF, ultimately influencing the policy positions of both policy elites and members of the public.

Recalling the expectations in H5, analytical results highlight general similarities between local policy elites and the general public but important differences. With regard to topical content of policy positions. Similar semantic patterning is found in the content of communications suggesting that both elites and the public rely on similar cognitive images to form broad policy positions on HF. They both recall damage to the environment in the form of water pollution and earthquakes. The policy positions of elites and the public also show similarities to independently recorded perspectives on environmental impacts (Stockton, 2015; US EPA National Center for Environmental Assessment and Frithsen, 2015;Vengosh et al., 2013). Differences relate to variations in positions on economic outcomes but the semantic patterns in respondents' policy positions are remarkably similar to communications that attribute HF with energy independence and job creation, (Federal Multiagency Collaboration on Unconventional Oil and Gas Research: A Strategy for Research and Development, 2014;Greenstone, 2018).

With regard to the effects attributable to cultural beliefs, results generally support theoretical expectations. Cultural beliefs were found to play a fundamental role in setting the context for policy-relevant judgments for both local policy elites and the general public.

Reflected in H6, higher levels of political sophistication possessed by policy elites are expected to ease the cognitive selection of contrasting outcomes associated with HF, resulting in a stronger effect of beliefs on policy positions among policy elites. The results partially support this expectation. A comparison of regression coefficients indicates that beliefs for policy elites who exhibit predispositions toward egalitarian and individualist beliefs have a slightly stronger effect when compared to the public. Knowledge has been identified as fundamental for processing political information in ways that reconcile with existing beliefs (Miller & Krosnick, 2000, p. 312) and could explain these results. As discussed earlier, the impact of knowledge on belief coherence is a topic for debate and is attributable to inconsistent operationalization. The approach adopted in this study does not define policy elites with direct or multidimensional measures of knowledge. Rather, it addresses a level of political sophistication possessed by local policy elites that more closely relates to an advanced knowledge of the political process as evidenced by their rise to a position of local political influence. This approach likely also captures the degree of domainspecific knowledge attained by local policy elites during the course of fulfilling their official duties given the demands to coordinate with other levels of government. Knowledge has been found to ease comprehension (Nelson et al., 1997;Nelson & Oxley, 1999;Zaller, 1985Zaller, , 1992) ) and the results align with expectations that higher levels of political sophistication ease cognition. It is possible that the policy elites in this study are no more knowledgeable on this specific issue than the general public. The inability to isolate the effects of multiple dimensions of knowledge is a limitation of this study but does point to an interesting direction for future research.

Practically, this study offers insight that may be useful for developing more effective communications around contested energy policy issues. While this study specifically focuses on the issue of HF, the identification of economic and environmental tradeoffs has been tied to public support for natural gas policies as well (Pierce et al., 2018).

This study contributes to ongoing theoretical development in the field of public policy in several ways. First, this work is intended to compliment ongoing scholarship focused on policy-relevant communication strategies. Acknowledgment of the cognitive process involved in forming policy positions arguably provides a more resolute understanding of the relationship between culturally biased beliefs and the formation of policy positions. Naturally, this approach highlights the way in which policy-relevant communications may be cognitively processed and provides some insight into why some communications might succeed or fail. The environmental and economic dimensions of HF are often embedded in strategic policy communications (Gottlieb et al., 2018) suggesting that individuals already intuitively understand how certain conceptual images can be applied to persuade. The approach taken in this study illustrates a method for examining how beliefs and cognition work together. Most importantly, this study indicates that culturally biased beliefs are instrumental in cognitively simplifying complex and controversial policy issues like HF. Furthermore, political sophistication likely strengthens the influence of beliefs in this process. These findings begin to examine how belief informed communications may be used by policy elites to exert influence over public policy positions (Baumgartner & Mahoney, 2008;Jones & Baumgartner, 2005), and collectively curate policy images within political institutions (Kirp, 1982) and subsystem coalitions (Weible et al., 2020) to expand interest within policy subsystems (Sabatier & Jenkins-Smith, 1999;True et al., 2007;Weible et al., 2009). Finally, belief congruent communications within HF subsystems at the meso-level have been traced to changes in policy (Heikkila et al., 2014). The results of this study suggest that affective processing may facilitate the organization of micro-level communications for meso-level use. This understanding is consistent with previous studies indicating that beliefs play a fundamental role in cognitive organization and processing of policy-relevant information (Jones & Song, 2014). Future research tying micro and meso-level processes could provide a more comprehensive understanding of how beliefs facilitate policy change.

Finally, this study offers some unique methodological approaches. First, it offers a rare opportunity to compare the attitudes of policy elites with the general public. Policy communications within a democratic framework involve complex interactions between the general public and elites and previous work suggests that members of the public have less influence on policy than economic elites and organized groups (Gilens & Page, 2014). Additionally, this study contributes a more nuanced perspective that expands on purely quantitative analysis. Although it is not without limitations, the use of open-ended survey questions to facilitate unstructured responses minimizes researcher-imposed constraints or biases. Of course, blank and short responses resulting from this approach may have important implications for the conclusions drawn from this study. Regardless, this approach provides a novel measure and relevant insights (Geer, 1991, p. 360) with results that are most valuable when combined with other relevant studies. Unsupervised machine learning techniques like the one used in this study to generate topics offer feasible approaches to examine attitudes on a much larger scale. The results shared here are meant to inspire further research into connections between cognitive policy images, policy attitudes, and policy communications. This could be helpful for developing testable hypotheses that further explore the process of narrative cognition (Jones et al., 2014), stakeholder strategies (Heikkila et al., 2014), or policy conflicts (Heikkila & Weible, 2017).

Previous studies have noted the contrasting views surrounding the use of HF and found evidence suggesting that positions on the issue commonly feature positive economic outcomes in the form of energy security, job creation, and regional economic growth contrasted with negative environmental and health outcomes. These opposing views are believed to sustain continued policy debates. Expanding on previous studies, this study examines factors emerging from policy process theories and theories of cognition to better understand how and why contrasting attributes of HF outcomes are incorporated into policy positions. By comparing local policy elites with the general public, this study provides a unique perspective with which to assess how political sophistication impacts individual-level judgment.

The analysis begins with an examination of the cognitive (mental) policy images used by policy elites and the general public to think and communicate about HF. Synthesizing theoretical frameworks applied to understand the policy process with theories of cognition, this study tests whether contrasting policy positions may emerge from processing inherent to cognition under conditions of uncertainty. Cognition distortion (Knapp & Beck, 2008) is a reliance on contrasting constructs to organize information (Berlin, 1990). It is affect-driven and a somewhat rigid way of processing that leverages bipolarized aspects of an issue in cognitively useful ways that support decision making (Bonfá-Araujo et al., 2021). The process involves using affective feelings to assign valence to mental images as a way to tag, store, and recall information. Recalling H1, this process of affect-driven cognition is expected to produce contrasting policy positions on HF which reflect the positive economic and negative environmental outcomes recorded in numerous previous studies. STM was used to extract latent topics using affective feelings toward HF and found that both policy elites and the general public use cognitive images to form broadly defined policy positions in similar ways. Based on these results, it follows that the uncertainty of outcomes from HF compels individuals to simplify their understanding of the issue using affect-driven cognition which directs individuals' selective attention toward contrasting economic and environmental dimensions of HF. As described earlier, context also plays an important role in this process and will be addressed in the following discussions around H2-H4.

While not the primary focus of the study, it is worth noting that in addition to contrasting economic and environmental policy concerns, the analysis also revealed the presence of nonpolarized, non-valenced attributes embedded within the respondents' policy positions. While much less common in its usage, the descriptive attributes of HF are also used to think about and communicate about HF. This result may hold some practical importance. The identification of uncontested areas of an issue are an important step in overcoming cognitive conflict and building consensus (Lubell, 2000). It is not possible to know from this study how neutral dimensions might factor into consensus building on HF but these results point to an interesting area for future inquiry

Although it is not without limitations, the use of open-ended survey questions to facilitate unstructured responses minimizes researcher-imposed constraints or biases. Of course, blank and short responses resulting from this approach may have important implications for the conclusions drawn from this study. Regardless, this approach provides a novel measure and relevant insights (Geer, 1991, p. 360) with results that are most valuable when combined with other relevant studies. Unsupervised machine learning techniques like the one used in this study to generate topics offer feasible approaches to examine attitudes on a much larger scale. The results shared here are meant to inspire further research into connections between cognitive policy images, policy attitudes, and policy communications. This could be helpful for developing testable hypotheses that further explore the process of narrative cognition (Jones et al., 2014), stakeholder strategies (Heikkila et al., 2014), or policy conflicts (Heikkila & Weible, 2017).
sections.preview_head: Previous studies have noted the contrasting views surrounding the use of HF and found evidence suggesting that positions on the issue commonly feature positive economic outcomes in the form of energy security, job creation, and regional economic growth contrasted with negative environmental and health outcomes. These opposing views are believed to sustain continued policy debates. Expanding on previous studies, this study examines factors emerging from policy process theories and theories of cognition to better understand how and why contrasting attributes of HF outcomes are incorporated into policy positions.

paragraphs: 1 With regard to the choice of cities included in this dataset, resources provided by organizations such as the chamber of commerce and municipal leagues for the respective states were mined and all available emails were extracted. Municipal leagues are self-proclaimed non-partisan organizations specializing in information relevant to local government. The cities or townships within each state that did not have public contact information available through the municipal league make up the most rural and/or least populated areas of the states examined which is a considerable limitation of this study. Conversely, the sample represents members of local government in the most densely populated and politically complex cities in each state. Similarly, resources provided by the state chapters of chamber of commerce provide a list of member organizations. These lists were mined for local chamber information. Chambers of commerce are membership-based organizations. For this reason, small businesses with limited resources are the least likely to be represented in this group and function as a limitation for this study. Similarly, the most successful and politically engaged businesses are most likely to be represented in this sample. Footnote 4 now acknowledges these limitations. 2 The number of latent topics may be specified by the researcher. K = 3 was applied to extract three latent topics for policy elites. Correlation analysis confirmed that each topic is unique in that it is uncorrelated with any of the other topics. The same procedure was applied and correlation analysis confirmed unique topics for the general public sample as well.

3 OLS regression was used to model the effects of all mentioned variables and interaction terms for elite and public beliefs to more directly test the effects of political sophistication on cognition. The results support that political sophistication as operationalized by inherent differences represented in these groups significantly affect how beliefs drive cognition at the level of p-value < .05.
